topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the coffee shop seemed to amplify the sudden, cold knot tightening in my stomach. Sarah was laughing, a bright, unrestrained sound, recounting a weekend trip. Then, she mentioned her 'new role' at the marketing firm, detailing spreadsheets and quarterly projections with such practiced confidence. I had seen the LinkedIn profile—a picture of her in a crisp blazer against a backdrop of gleaming glass. It didn't match the story she was spinning. My mouth went dry. I kept nodding, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face, while my eyes darted towards the exit, searching for an escape route. When she finally paused, waiting for my input, the silence stretched, thick and suffocating. I just managed a weak, "That sounds... busy," my voice sounding unnaturally high.

[story 2]

I watched Mark pace by the window, his shoulders hunched, the tension in his jaw palpable. He’d been so enthusiastic about landing that senior position at the tech startup. Now, the reality of it—that he was still working the night shift at the diner, the one he’d conveniently omitted from our conversations—was crashing down on me. I fiddled incessantly with the rim of my water glass, the condensation cold against my fingertips. Every time he glanced my way, I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and undeniable. "So, how's the pitch meeting going?" I asked, trying to keep my tone light, casual. His eyes flickered away, a quick, furtive movement that spoke volumes. He just shrugged, a gesture that felt too small, too dismissive. The air around him seemed to thicken, and I suddenly wanted the floor to swallow me whole.

[story 3]

It happened over lukewarm takeout. Liam was describing his career trajectory, waxing poetic about his ascent in the architectural field. He spoke of blueprints and high-rise designs with an air of deep satisfaction. I felt a sudden, sharp awareness of the discrepancy between his words and the vague, hurried way he handled his phone when I asked about his daily routine. A wave of awkwardness washed over me, making my palms instantly slick. I tried to focus on the food, concentrating on the texture of the noodles, but my attention kept snagging on his evasive answers. When he finally steered the conversation away, his smile didn't quite reach his eyes; it was a strained thing. I cleared my throat, the sound embarrassingly loud in the quiet space, and mumbled something about needing to check an email, suddenly desperate to disappear into the background.
